title: Maps
summary: The technology of representing spatial relationships on a surface — one of the oldest cognitive and political tools in human history, a technology that does not merely describe territory but creates it: setting borders, asserting sovereignty, enabling navigation, organizing colonial space, and shaping how people understand their place in the world.
hook: Every map is an argument. The Mercator projection — still the default in most online mapping applications — makes Europe and North America appear much larger than they are and Africa much smaller. This is not an error: it was a useful distortion for navigation. But when it became the default image of the world in schools and offices, it encoded a geography of relative importance that had nothing to do with area. Maps have always been made from somewhere by someone for some purpose. Understanding them requires asking who made them, who they were made for, and what they were made to do.

beginner.theFoundation:
Every map involves choices: what to include, what to omit, where to place the center, how to handle the distortion that comes from representing a spherical world on a flat surface. These choices are never purely technical. They reflect the values, purposes, and power of the people who make them.

To map something is to make a claim about it. To put a boundary on a map is to assert that the boundary is real. To name a place on a map — or to leave it unnamed — is to make a judgment about who counts and who doesn't. Maps are not neutral records of the world. They are arguments about the world.

The oldest surviving map is a clay tablet from Babylon dating to around 600 BCE. Maps appear in almost every complex civilization — ancient Egyptian land surveys, Greek geographical drawings, Roman road maps, Chinese topographic surveys, Islamic world maps, Polynesian stick charts. Mapping is as old as civilization itself, and so is the use of maps to assert power.
beginner.howItArrived:
European cartography underwent a fundamental transformation in the 15th century. The recovery of Ptolemy's Geography gave Renaissance cartographers mathematical tools for projecting the spherical earth onto a flat surface. The Portuguese and Spanish voyages generated new data. Within a century of Columbus's first voyage, European mapmakers had produced roughly accurate world maps incorporating the Americas.

But the standard European narrative — of steady cartographic progress from rough sketches to accurate maps — misses what happened everywhere else. Chinese cartography was already sophisticated. The Islamic tradition had produced world maps more accurate than any contemporary European equivalent. Polynesian navigators read the Pacific through stick charts and star paths. Maps are not a European invention. The European tradition's eventual dominance reflects its connection to colonial expansion, not its superior accuracy.

Gerardus Mercator's projection (1569) — which preserved compass-bearing angles, making it ideal for navigation — became the dominant world map and has shaped how the world has been visualized ever since.
beginner.whatItReorganized:
Maps reorganize political reality by creating visible boundaries. Before modern cartography, political authority was understood through personal relationships — lords and vassals, tribute and allegiance — rather than through control over a precisely bounded territory. The modern state's claim to sovereignty over a defined space was enabled and expressed by maps that showed exactly where that space ended.

Colonial cartography extended this logic globally. European powers mapped territories they were claiming — often before exploring them, filling unknown spaces with 'terra incognita.' These maps asserted ownership through representation. To put a territory on a European map, under a European name, was to claim it. The act of mapping preceded the act of governing, and helped make governing possible.
beginner.thePoliticalEconomy:
Cadastral maps — detailed surveys of who owns which land — are among the most consequential maps ever made. The enclosure of English commons in the 16th through 19th centuries was preceded by cadastral surveys that identified common land for privatization. Colonial land appropriation in Africa, the Americas, and Asia was preceded by surveys that classified indigenous land as 'empty' or 'waste.' The map did not just record the transfer of land — it enabled it.

Contemporary property law rests on cadastral mapping. The boundary on the map determines who owns what and who pays taxes on it. To exist outside the cadastral system — to have land that is not mapped, not bounded, not formally owned — is to be invisible to the state and therefore to be at risk of dispossession.
beginner.theFeedback:
Maps shape what people believe about the world. National maps that show a country's full territorial claims — even when disputed — perform a political act every time they are displayed in a school. Chinese maps showing the 'nine-dash line' in the South China Sea assert claims that neighbors reject. Indian and Pakistani maps of Kashmir show different boundaries. The map on the school wall shapes what children grow up believing the world looks like.

This is why map projections become political arguments. The Mercator projection — designed for navigation — inflates the apparent size of high-latitude landmasses. Europe looks large. Africa looks small. An equal-area projection shows Africa is actually larger than Europe and the United States combined. The projection choice seems technical. Its effects are political.
beginner.presentAndFuture:
Digital mapping — Google Maps, GPS, satellite imagery, geographic information systems — has made spatial data more accurate, more accessible, and more ubiquitous than ever. It has also made mapping more commercial and less public. The maps most people use are produced by private companies whose primary purposes are advertising revenue and logistics.

The counter-mapping movement uses digital tools to create maps representing indigenous and marginalized communities — showing land use patterns, sacred sites, and customary boundaries that official maps erase. In disaster response, humanitarian mappers have created detailed infrastructure maps within hours of a crisis. But the basic questions mapping always raises — who makes maps, who they serve, whose geography they represent — have not been resolved by going digital.

general.theFoundation:
J.B. Harley's 'Deconstructing the Map' (1989) is the foundational text of critical cartography — the approach that treats maps not as objective representations of reality but as social constructions embedding power relations. Harley argued that maps exercise power through their silences as much as their inclusions. What is absent from a map — unnamed settlements, unrecognized boundaries, erased indigenous place names — is as politically significant as what appears. Drawing on Foucault, the French philosopher of knowledge and power, he argued that cartographic knowledge and political authority are inseparable: the power to name and bound is the power to control.

Harley's claim has been contested. Some critics argue it reduces maps to political instruments and ignores their genuine representational achievements. Mathematical accuracy in mapping — the ability to represent coastlines and distances with precision — is a real epistemic achievement that the political critique can undervalue. The productive tension between maps as knowledge and maps as power has driven critical cartography for three decades.
general.howItArrived:
The Islamic cartographic tradition challenges the standard European narrative of cartographic progress. Al-Idrisi's world map (1154), produced for Roger II of Sicily, shows a more accurate representation of the Old World than any contemporary European map. It was drawn with south at the top — a convention reflecting the Islamic tradition's different orientation toward Mecca and the Arabian Peninsula. The Islamic tradition drew on Ptolemy but extended it substantially, incorporating Arab geographical knowledge from trading networks spanning the Indian Ocean to West Africa.

Jerry Brotton's A History of the World in Twelve Maps examines how each of twelve maps — from Ptolemy to Google Earth — reveals the assumptions and priorities of the civilization that produced it. His account shows that cartographic development was not a linear European progress narrative but a series of distinct traditions — Chinese, Islamic, Polynesian, European — each with different organizing principles, different centers, and different purposes.
general.whatItReorganized:
Matthew Edney's Mapping an Empire (1997) is the essential study of colonial cartography as a political project. The Survey of India (1767–1843) was simultaneously the most rigorous geographical project of its era — producing genuinely accurate maps through painstaking triangulation — and an assertion of British sovereignty through systematic measurement. Edney's argument: the survey did not merely represent British India, it helped constitute it. The act of measuring and bounding made the subcontinent legible as a unified administrative unit in ways that served imperial governance.

The broader implication is that modern states — colonial and otherwise — were not simply mapped after the fact but were partly produced through cartographic practices. The boundary on the map did not reflect a pre-existing political reality; it helped create that reality by making it visible, administrable, and defensible. This applies to post-colonial states too, most of whose borders derive from colonial cartographic decisions made with minimal attention to existing political or ethnic boundaries.
general.thePoliticalEconomy:
The counter-mapping movement uses community-based cartography to challenge official maps and assert indigenous land claims. Nancy Peluso's work in Indonesia documented how local communities created maps showing customary forest use that was invisible to official forestry management maps. They then used those maps as tools in land tenure disputes with the state. In Canada and the United States, indigenous land mapping has been used in treaty rights litigation: the map that shows continuous historical use of a territory is legal evidence of a claim.

Counter-mapping shows that cartographic tools are not inherently instruments of the powerful. The same practices of measurement, boundary-drawing, and representation that enabled colonial dispossession can be repurposed to resist it. But counter-mapping also faces structural limitations. Indigenous maps must be legible within the legal and bureaucratic frameworks of the states they are contesting. This means adopting the conventions of those frameworks — discrete bounded parcels, written documentation, coordinate systems — even when those conventions distort indigenous spatial relationships.
general.theFeedback:
The Peters projection controversy of the 1970s and 1980s illustrates how map projections become sites of political argument. Arno Peters promoted his equal-area projection as 'fair to all peoples.' He argued that the Mercator's inflation of high latitudes — making Europe and North America appear larger relative to equatorial regions — encoded a Eurocentric worldview in the default image of the world. The argument had political traction: the Peters projection was adopted by several UN agencies and development organizations.

Professional cartographers rejected Peters's claims on technical grounds — his projection has its own distortions, elongating tropical landmasses vertically, and the Mercator was designed for navigation, not comparative area representation. The controversy raised a genuine question cartographers had not adequately addressed: if no projection is distortion-free, what criteria should govern which distortion is acceptable, and who decides? The professional response — that accuracy on the relevant variable matters — is correct but sidesteps the political question of whose relevant variable counts.
general.presentAndFuture:
OpenStreetMap — the collaborative open-source mapping project founded in 2004 — represents the most ambitious attempt to democratize cartographic production. Its coverage in many regions of the Global South exceeds that of Google Maps, because local contributors have mapped roads, buildings, and facilities that commercial services don't find worth mapping. Humanitarian mapping teams mobilized through the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team have created critical infrastructure maps for disaster response in Haiti, the Philippines, and elsewhere within hours of a crisis.

But OpenStreetMap also illustrates the limits of crowdsourced cartography. Coverage reflects contributor distribution: dense in wealthy urban areas, sparse in rural and poor regions. Naming disputes — whether the body of water between Japan and Korea is the Sea of Japan or the East Sea, whether the Falklands appear as Malvinas — reproduce geopolitical conflicts in the map database. The governance of a global commons map, where contributors from opposing nations disagree about basic geographic facts, is an unsolved problem.

advanced.theFoundation: Denis Wood's The Power of Maps (1992) extends Harley's analysis through detailed examination of a single map to show how maps present the historical — products of specific human choices — as the natural, the inevitable. The boundary on the map looks like a feature of the landscape rather than a political decision. Maps naturalize social constructions by presenting them as spatial facts.
advanced.howItArrived: The relationship between cartography and the Scientific Revolution — how mathematical geography contributed to the broader transformation of European knowledge — has been analyzed by David Turnbull and Steven Harris. The standardization of measurement, development of accurate projection mathematics, and use of surveys all involved the same epistemological commitments: quantification, systematic observation, reproducibility. But cartography had political and economic drivers other sciences lacked.
advanced.whatItReorganized: The Selden Map — a Chinese map of maritime Asia, probably produced in the early 17th century and rediscovered in the Bodleian Library in 2008 — illustrates how different cartographic traditions construct space differently. Centered on China's southern coast with trade routes as organizing principle, it reflects a Chinese maritime commercial perspective rather than a European territorial sovereignty perspective. Timothy Brook's Mr. Selden's Map of China uses it to analyze how maps reveal not just geography but cosmology.
advanced.thePoliticalEconomy: The contemporary geopolitics of digital mapping shows that cartographic politics have not been resolved by digitization. Google Maps displays different borders in different countries: in India, Kashmir appears entirely Indian; in Pakistan, it shows the Line of Control. China requires all map services to use an offset coordinate system (GCJ-02), making foreign mapping applications inaccurate within China. The politics of digital cartography are the old politics of sovereignty played out in coordinate systems and API protocols.
advanced.theFeedback: The field of indigenous cartography — documentation and recovery of pre-colonial spatial knowledge — has both scholarly and political dimensions. The History of Cartography project challenged the Eurocentric narrative that systematic cartography was a European invention. But the project also raised questions about what counts as a 'map' — whether oral geographical knowledge, stick charts, and memory maps qualify — and whose criteria of inclusion are applied.
advanced.presentAndFuture: Volunteered geographic information (VGI) — spatial data contributed through GPS, mobile phones, and social media — has transformed the data landscape. OpenStreetMap contains local detail government and commercial maps lack in many regions. But VGI data has systematic biases: it overrepresents wealthy, urban, English-speaking populations. The gap between the ubiquity of digital spatial data and its unequal representation is one of the defining equity challenges in contemporary geography.

foundation.theStory: Maps matter as one of the oldest and most consequential technologies in human history — tools that enable navigation, organize political territory, assert sovereignty, and shape how people understand their place in the world. Understanding them requires moving beyond maps as neutral representations to maps as political arguments.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern whether maps are neutral representations or political arguments, the relationship between colonial cartography and colonial possession, how map projections encode cultural biases, whether digital mapping has democratized or concentrated cartographic power, and how indigenous spatial knowledge should be recognized.
foundation.whyItMatters: Maps matter for understanding navigation, colonial expansion, the modern state system, property law, national identity, the Scientific Revolution, and the contemporary politics of territory and borders.
questions: The Mercator projection makes Europe appear larger than Africa. Arno Peters argued this reflected Eurocentric bias; cartographers argued it was a navigational tool being misused. Is a map projection politically biased if it was designed for a different purpose? Who is responsible for the political effects of a technical choice?

Colonial cartography mapped territories before they were explored, asserting ownership through representation. Contemporary international law recognizes territory based partly on historical maps. Does this give historical cartographic claims legal force — and if so, whose historical maps should count?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: J.B. Harley's The New Nature of Maps is the essential foundation of critical cartography. Denis Wood's The Power of Maps is the most accessible account. Jerry Brotton's A History of the World in Twelve Maps provides the accessible narrative history. Timothy Brook's Mr. Selden's Map of China is the best case study. For colonial cartography, Matthew Edney's Mapping an Empire is the standard account.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: Analysis of historical maps requires both technical expertise (understanding projection systems, surveying methods) and interpretive sensitivity (reading maps as cultural artifacts). The main limitation is the fragile and incomplete survival of early maps. For contemporary maps, the primary challenge is understanding the algorithmic and commercial decisions that determine what appears in digital services.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about maps must engage with the critical cartography tradition — taking a position on whether Harley's claim that all maps are political arguments is too strong or too weak. It must address whether colonial cartography constituted colonial possession or merely reflected it. And it must engage with whether digital maps are more or less politically charged than their paper predecessors.
questions: OpenStreetMap allows anyone to contribute to a collaborative world map — but also allows anyone to delete data, insert fake roads, or rename places to assert territorial claims. Does democratizing cartographic production make maps more or less reliable as representations of reality?

China requires all digital maps within its territory to use an offset coordinate system that makes foreign applications inaccurate. This is described as a security measure. Who does this serve, and what does it reveal about the relationship between digital cartography and state sovereignty?

research.topic: The relationship between colonial cartography and territorial possession
research.content: Scholars including Edney, Harley, and the post-colonial geography tradition have established that mapping was both a tool and expression of colonial possession — that placing territory on a European map asserted sovereignty recognized by other European powers, that colonial surveys made territories legible for extraction, and that categories used by colonial maps ('empty,' 'terra nullius') provided legal justifications for indigenous dispossession.

research.topic: The politics and accuracy of digital mapping services
research.content: Political geographers have documented systematic patterns in how Google, Apple, and other commercial mapping services handle contested territories — displaying different borders for different national audiences, handling indigenous place names inconsistently, and prioritizing commercial over public geographic information. The relationship between digital mapping accuracy and social equity — systematic gaps in coverage for low-income and non-English-speaking communities — is also an active research area.

research.topic: The spatial knowledge systems of pre-literate societies
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: systematic documentation of spatial knowledge, navigation methods, and territorial understanding of pre-literate societies comparable in detail to the documentary record for literate civilizations. Why unavailable: pre-literate spatial knowledge was transmitted orally and through practice; it was often suppressed or replaced by colonial education; and people who possessed it were displaced or killed before documentation. What can be established: partial documentation from anthropological fieldwork and surviving communities. What cannot be established: the full extent and sophistication of spatial knowledge systems destroyed by colonial processes.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Progressive Map
comparativeNarrative.content: The scientific tradition reads mapping history as progressive improvement — from rough, speculative representations to accurate, mathematically rigorous ones. On this reading, the politics of maps are secondary to their technical accuracy. The Peters projection controversy illustrates the limits of this view: an equal-area projection may be accurate in one dimension while distorting in others.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Political Map
comparativeNarrative.content: The critical tradition reads maps as political arguments embedded in apparently neutral representations. Every map chooses a center, a perspective, a scale, and a set of categories. Every map naturalizes historical and political choices as geographic facts. The most important questions about any map: who made it, for whom, and for what purpose?

comparativeNarrative.name: Other Geographies
comparativeNarrative.content: The indigenous tradition challenges the assumption that European mathematical cartography is the standard against which all other spatial representations should be measured. Polynesian stick charts, Aboriginal songlines, Inuit mental maps, and indigenous land use maps represent sophisticated spatial knowledge systems serving different purposes. Their recovery challenges the narrative of cartographic progress.

rabbitHole.label: Navigation
rabbitHole.reason: Maps and navigation developed together — the demand for accurate navigational charts drove the development of mathematical cartography, and improved maps enabled more ambitious navigation.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: Colonial cartography was both a tool of colonial expansion and an expression of colonial sovereignty — asserting possession through the act of representation.

rabbitHole.label: Empire
rabbitHole.reason: Every empire has required maps — to administer territory, plan military campaigns, coordinate resource extraction, and assert the spatial extent of sovereign authority.

rabbitHole.label: The Scientific Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: Mathematical cartography — the development of projection systems, geodetic surveying, and coordinate systems — was both a product of and contributor to the Scientific Revolution's commitment to quantification.

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: The national map — showing the nation's territory as a bounded, colored space — is one of the most powerful symbols of national identity and a primary tool through which national territory is constructed and contested.

rabbitHole.label: Paper
rabbitHole.reason: Paper enabled reproduction and distribution of maps at scale — before paper and printing, maps were rare expensive objects; after, they could be produced in quantity and widely distributed.

rabbitHole.label: The Silk Road
rabbitHole.reason: The Silk Road's routes were mapped by Arab and Chinese geographers centuries before European cartography — al-Idrisi's world map reflects Islamic geographical knowledge of the trade routes connecting China to the Mediterranean.

rabbitHole.label: The Internet
rabbitHole.reason: Digital mapping has transformed how spatial information is produced and accessed; the politics of digital cartography — whose map of a contested territory is 'correct' — are the old politics of sovereignty in new form.

reference.author: Harley, J.B.
reference.title: The New Nature of Maps: Essays in the History of Cartography
reference.annotation: The foundational text of critical cartography — Harley's essays establishing maps as political arguments transformed how geographers and historians analyze cartographic sources.

reference.author: Brotton, Jerry
reference.title: A History of the World in Twelve Maps
reference.annotation: The best accessible history — Brotton's account of twelve maps spanning the ancient world to Google Earth shows how each reveals the values of its era; the ideal starting point for general readers.

reference.author: Wood, Denis
reference.title: The Power of Maps
reference.annotation: The essential critical account — Wood's analysis of how maps exercise power is the most accessible entry into critical cartography.

reference.author: Edney, Matthew
reference.title: Mapping an Empire: The Geographical Construction of British India
reference.annotation: The essential colonial cartography account — Edney shows the inseparability of scientific and political purposes in colonial mapping.

reference.author: Harley, J.B.
reference.title: The New Nature of Maps: Essays in the History of Cartography
reference.annotation: The foundational critical account of maps as power — Harley's essays showed that maps are never neutral representations but always encode the interests, values, and blind spots of their makers. Transformed the history of cartography from a technical to a cultural and political discipline.

commerce.title: A History of the World in Twelve Maps
commerce.author: Jerry Brotton
commerce.note: Start here — the most readable cartography history; Brotton shows how each of twelve maps reveals the values of its era; no specialist knowledge required.

commerce.title: The Power of Maps
commerce.author: Denis Wood
commerce.note: The essential critical account — Wood's analysis of how maps exercise power through silences as much as content; more accessible than Harley and the best introduction to critical cartography.

commerce.title: How to Lie with Maps
commerce.author: Mark Monmonier
commerce.note: The essential practical guide — Monmonier's account of how maps distort, mislead, and manipulate teaches you to read any map critically; genuinely enjoyable.

commerce.title: Mr. Selden's Map of China
commerce.author: Timothy Brook
commerce.note: The best case study — Brook uses one rediscovered 17th-century Chinese map to illuminate an entirely different spatial imagination; beautifully written.

commerce.title: Prisoners of Geography
commerce.author: Tim Marshall
commerce.note: The most accessible account of how geography shapes politics — Marshall's analysis of how rivers, mountains, and coasts determine what nations can do; the right entry for readers who want the political science before the cartography.

popularCulture.title: Google Maps and the politics of digital cartography
popularCulture.description: Google Maps has made the politics of cartography more visible than ever. Google displays different borders in different countries: Kashmir appears entirely Indian in India; Pakistan's version shows the Line of Control. Google Maps is not a neutral representation of the world but a product of commercial and political negotiations — a fact now visible to anyone who notices maps of the same place look different depending on where you're viewing them from.

popularCulture.title: The Map of Tiny Perfect Things — Ian Samuels (2021)
popularCulture.description: This teen romantic fantasy uses cartography as a metaphor for attention: the act of noticing, recording, and cherishing what would otherwise be invisible. Counter-maps, indigenous land maps, and personal maps all share this quality — they encode what the mapmaker found significant, which is always a statement of values as much as a description of space.

popularCulture.title: 'Here be dragons' and unknown space
popularCulture.description: 'Here be dragons' — which appears on exactly one known historical map, the Lenox Globe of around 1510 — has become universal shorthand for unknown and dangerous space. In contemporary use it appears in software documentation, organizational strategy, and everyday speech. It captures the experience of standing at the edge of what is known and confronting what isn't.

popularCulture.title: The Peters projection debate
popularCulture.description: Arno Peters's campaign in the 1970s–80s to replace the Mercator projection — arguing its distortions made Europe and North America appear larger than Africa and South America, reinforcing Eurocentric worldviews — was the first time map projections became a major public controversy. The controversy made visible what cartographers had always known: choosing a world map is choosing a worldview.

popularCulture.title: Piranesi — Susanna Clarke (2020)
popularCulture.description: Clarke's novel — set in an infinite labyrinthine House mapped obsessively by its lone inhabiting narrator — uses cartography as an act of sanity-preservation: the protagonist maps the House because mapping is the only way to maintain orientation in an environment designed to disorient. The novel suggests that all maps are partial, personal, and indispensable precisely because of rather than despite their partiality.
